editionStmt.edition: Digital Critical Edition of the Chapter Summaries (Capitula) to Genesis in the Tours Bibles

publicationStmt.p:
Welcome to the digital critical edition project of the Capitula to the Octateuch in the Tours Bibles!
The editions are part of the PRIN 2022 PNRR project DOBiPS – Data Oriented Biblical Paratext Studies , awarded for the 2023–2025 period to the research units at the Universities of Udine (P.I. E. Colombi) and Cassino (P.I. R. Casavecchia), under the competitive PRIN PNRR call – National Recovery and Resilience Plan, Mission 4 "Education and Research", funded by the European Union’s NextGenerationEU programme (protocol no. P2022ZW4AW).
The Capitula prefixed to the Octateuch in the Tours Bibles have been encoded in XML/TEI and published using the software EVT (Edition Visualization Technology).
The editions were prepared by members of the Udine research unit: Emanuela Colombi, Margherita Filippozzi, and Silverio Franzoni.
Details of the encoding choices and related methodological reflections have been presented in the working paper: E. Colombi, M. Filippozzi, S. Franzoni, The Digital Critical Edition of the Capitula to the Octateuch in the Tours Bibles , DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.16761173 .
Information on the individual editions is available by clicking the 'info' button at the top of the left-hand pane.

bibl.author: Donatien de Bruyne
bibl.title: Sommaires, divisions et rubriques de la Bible latine

bibl.author: Bernhard Bischoff
bibl.title: Katalog der festländischen Handschriften des neunten Jahrhunderts (mit Ausnahme der wisigotischen)

bibl.author: Bonifatius Fischer
bibl.title: Vetus Latina: die Reste der altlateinischen Bibel. 2 Genesis

msDesc.head: Biblia latina, Pentateuchus. 1st volume of Maurdramnus' Bible

msItem.title: capitula Genesis supplied in the XIIth century

supportDesc.extent:
II + 351 folios
height 352 mm width 260 mm

source.p: Before the Alcuin's Bibles according to Fischer's classification (p. 256).

msDesc.head: Biblia latina Veteris et Novi Testamenti

source.p: First period of the Turonian production according to Fischer's classification (p. 257).

supportDesc.extent:
II + 214 folios
height 405 mm width 295 mm

handDesc.handNote: Carolingian minuscule by different hands (at least five)

source.p: Second period of the Turonian production according to Fischer's classification (p. 259).
Second period of the Turonian production according to Fischer's classification (p. 261).

msDesc.head: Biblia latina Veteris et Novi Testamenti

source.p: Third period of the Turonian production under Fridugisus (periode II) according to Fischer 's classification (p. 261).

msDesc.head: Biblia latina Veteris et Novi Testamenti

source.p: Fourth period of the Turonian production under Fridugisus (periode II) according to Fischer's classification (p. 262).

msDesc.head: Biblia latina Veteris et Novi Testamenti

origin.origPlace: Tours, Abbey St. Martin or Marmoutier (Fischer, p. 263)

source.p: Fourth period of the Turonian production under Fridugisus (periode II) according to Fischer's classification (p. 263).

msDesc.head: Biblia latina Veteris et Novi Testamenti

source.p: Second period of the Turonian production under Adelard (periode III) according to Fischer's classification (p. 264).

msDesc.head: Biblia latina Veteris et Novi Testamenti (The Moutier-Grandval Bible)

source.p: Third period of the Turonian production according to Fischer's classification (pp. 264-265).

msDesc.head: Biblia latina Veteris et Novi Testamenti (Alcuin's Bible)

source.p: Third period of the Turonian production according to Fischer's classification (p. 265).

msDesc.head: Biblia latina (First Bible of Charles the Bald)

supportDesc.extent:
I + 423 folios
height 495 mm width 375 mm

source.p: Fourth period (Abbot Vivian, 843-851) of the Turonian production according to Fischer's classification (p. 267).

msDesc.head: Biblia Latina Veteris et Novi Testamenti

supportDesc.extent:
I + 382 + I folios
height 495 mm width 350 mm

source.p: Fifth period (853-c.900) of the Turonian production according to Fischer's classification (p. 268).

listWit.witness:
text of the A series as printed by D. de Bruyne, pp. 2-8.
digital facsimile

tagsDecl.rendition: the digital reproduction is b/w, impossible to determine color

handNotes.handNote: uncertainty whether in the scribe’s hand or added later

front.p: This digital edition presents the reconstruction of the capitula of Series A (according to Donatien de Bruyne's classification ) that precede the Book of Genesis, in the textual form known from the Tours scriptorium.

item.title: De e die primo primo die in quo lux facta est ??t

app.witDetail: The rubricator was about to write erroneously "II[I]", but stopped it (only the top of the line is drawn).

item.title: De e die secundo in quo caelum celum ca??um factum est
De e die tertio IIIo in quo terra ??rra et mare

De e die de die quarto IIIIo in quo sol et luna et e et
stellae stelle stellȩ stellas

De e die quinto quint? in quo repentia in in
mari et uolatilia caeli celi cæli

De e die sexto VIto sex?? VI in quo
quo
quadrupedia quatrupedia et serpentes et
et
et bestiae bestie bestiȩ
bestiȩ
bestiae et omnia pecora et repentia et homo

De e die septimo VIImo VII in quo requieuit requit omni ab omni The variant reading in the Tours Octateuch aligns with the Vulgate's text of Gn 2:2-3 requievit die septimo ab universo opere [...] cessaverat ab omni opere suo, that corresponds to the Greek ἀπὸ πάντων τῶν ἔργων αὐτοῦ (as attested, the Old Latin version closely follows the Greek phrasing: ab omnibus operibus suis). Φ Am1 (adiaph.) vs Tu opere consummato deus
De e hoc libro in quo uniuersa quae que facta sunt dicit

Vbi ni praeceptum preceptum The scribe abbreviated p̄ceptū and seems unfamiliar with using the diphthong. pre praeceptum The scribe was about to write "pre[ceptum]", but stopped to amend it. praecept?? præceptum dat dominus deus The Sg1 scribe likely confused the abbreviated forms ds̄ and dn̄s. However, cf. the Vulgate text at Gn 3:1 cur praecepit vobis deus (ὁ θεός/ deus is also found in the Greek and Old Latin versions). d?? adae ade et mulieri mulie?? eius ???s
Vbi bi seduxit serpens mulierem
Vbi bi interrogantur de transgresso transgressu transgressu

Educuntur aducuntur ducuntur educentur educuntur de paradyso paradiso
ne sumant de ligno vitae vite
Nati ati sunt adae ade filii cain et abel

app.witDetail: The scribe added the chapter heading that had been erroneously skipped in the interlinear space.

item.title: Adae ade dae natus est seth sedh sed pro abel
De e generationibus hominum

Enoch enoc noch placuit deo et transtulit eum illum The alternative readings eum and illum appear to be attested in the European forms of the Old Latin text (Gn 5:24 transtulit illum/ eum deus), whereas eum is likely the reading of the Vulgate. However, since illum is attested only as a singular reading within the Turonian corpus, it cannot be ruled out that this is an innovation of L1, unconnected to the Old Latin tradition. deus

Acceperunt ecceperunt cceperunt sibi uxores filii dei angeli fili dei filii dei fili dei
The European tradition of the Old Latin text appears to preserve both angeli dei and filii dei. This divergence reflects the Greek textual tradition, where some witnesses (e.g. Codex Alexandrinus) read οἱ ἄγγελοι, while the majority of Septuagint manuscripts read οἱ υἱοὶ (cf. Aug., civ. 15.23 Et septuaginta quidem interpretes et angelos dei dixerunt istos et filios dei). The Turonian corpus, however, unanimously adopts the Vulgate's filii dei (which appears in a corrupted form as fili in Sg1, P8, Z p.c.). This alignment with Jerome's Vulgate is consistent with his explicit rejection of the reading angeli (cf. Hier., in Is. XVIII [806A] acceperunt eas, non angeli, sed filii dei)." Φ (adiaph.) vs Am1 Tu et iratus est deus

Noe oe placuit deo et praecepit precepit The scribe abbreviated "p̄cepit" and seems unfamiliar with using the diphthong.
illi deus ut faceret arcam

Et t ingressus est noe in arcam arca The use of in with the ablative case alongside the verb ingredior is a later attested usage (cf. ThLL, 1568, s.v.). Φ (adiaph.) vs Am1 Tu et factum est diluuium diliuium

Memoratus emoratus moratus memoratus est dominus noe et eorum de his
While the text attested by the Tours Octateuch is acceptable, the additions in the Turonian corpus and in the Maurdramnus’ Bible (which appear to be independent of each other, with the former being preferable to the latter) may result from an intent to summarize collectively the animals with Noah in the Ark, as mentioned in the Biblical text. This is found in the Vulgate (Gn 8:1 cunctarumque animantium et omnium iumentorum) and, according to Fischer (Vetus Latina: die Reste..., Vol. 2 Genesis, Freiburg, 1951-1954), in the Old Latin version circulating in Italy, as attested by Ambrose (Am., Noe 56: et bestiarum et iumentorum).
Φ (adiaph.) vs Am1 vs Tu qui cum illo erant et siccata siccita est aqua

Anno nno sexcentesimo DC DCmo DCm et uno et uno primo The variant added by a corrector's hand in Sg1, which amends the lemma unanimously attested by the three traditional branches, was likely suggested by the parallel passage in the Vulgate (Gn 8:13: igitur sescentesimo primo anno). noe
noe exiuit exiit exiiut
exiuit noe The different word order in the Maurdramnus' Bible closely mirrors that of the Septuagint (Gn 8:18 καὶ ἐξῆλθεν Νωε). Φ Tu (adiaph.) vs Am1 de arca ipse
ipse et qui erant cum eo cum eo erant The word order in the Maurdramnus' Bible (and Z within the Turonian corpus) was likely influenced by the nearly identical phrasing of the preceding chapter heading. Φ praeter Z + Tu (adiaph.) vs Am1

Benedixit enedixit dominus noe et filios The scribe left a blank space (ca. 4 letters) after "filios"; it is impossible to determine whether a word was erased here.
eius

app.witDetail: The scribe added the chapter heading that had been erroneously skipped in the interlinear space.

item.title:
De e tribus
Φ Tu vs Am1 (error) filiis noe sem cham cam cham et iafeth iaphet iapheth Φ Am1 (adiaph.) vs Tu et de ipso ubi inebriatus inaebriatus est

De e generationibus generatioibus The scribe inadvertently omitted a tilde marking the abbreviation -n.
noe et filiorum eius

De e commixtione commixto commixtione The scribe was about to write "commixto[ne]" by mistake, but corrected it.
linguarum
De e generationibus thare tharȩ tharæ patris abraham abram abraham abram abrah abram abram abraham

Dixit ixit dominus ad ad
The expunction marks appear to have been erased, because they would have referred to the next chapter heading (see below).
abraham abram abraham abram abram abraham exi de terra tua

Visus isus est dominus deus
ad
abrahae abrahe abrae abrahae abrae ad locum in terra ad locum
sychem sichem Φ praeter Z + Tu (adiaph.) vs Am1

Promissiones romissiones abrahae abrahe abrae abrahȩ abrae abrahe et semini eius
statum sodomorum

Pugnauerunt ugnauerunt reges quattuor quatuor IIII IIIIor
aduersus aversus quinque V et coeperunt ceperunt coeperunt ceperunt acceperunt loth et omnem omne equitatum aequitatum sodomorum eius sodomorum The erroneous variant in Sg1 - likely corrected by the main hand itself - may have originated due to interference from the Vulgate text (Gn 14:12 nec non et Loth et substantiam eius).

app.lem:
dominus abrahae
abrahe abrae abrahȩ abrae abrahȩ et benedixit eum et dixit ei
app.rdg: dominus abrahe et benedixit eum et dixit ei

app.witDetail: The scribe added the chapter heading that had been erroneously skipped in the interlinear space and in the blank space at the end of ch. 31.

item.title: Visus isus est dominus abrahae abrahe et dedit dicit dedit ei signum sygnum Φ Tu (recte) vs Am1 circumcisionis et de isaac ysaac ipsa ac Φ (recte) vs Am1 vs Tu

app.witDetail: The scribe added the chapter heading that had been erroneously skipped in the interlinear space.

item.title: Venerunt enerunt ue????nt angeli in sodomis ??domis et manserunt apud loth lot The orthography of the Hebrew name in the Maurdramnus' Bible more closely resembles that of the Greek Λωτ. Φ Tu vs Am1
Pluit luit p???? dominus d?? ignem ????m et sulphur surpur sulphur et euertit uertit euertit sodomam

Locutus oqutus oquutus locut?? loquutus est
dominus ad abimelech ali abimelech The scribe may have initially begun to write "ali[melech]" (or something similar) by mistake, but corrected it.
pro sarra saraa sarraa

Sarra arra saraa sarraa peperit filium et uocauit eum eu?
eum pater eius pater eius eum Φ Tu (adiaph.) vs Am1 isaac isahac Φ Tu vs Am1
Dixit ixit sarra saraa sarraa ad abraham abraam proice ancillam ancilla Φ Am1 (recte) vs Tu et filium eius
Iurauerunt urauerunt inuicem abraham et abimelech abib abimelech The scribe was about to write "abib[imelech]" (or something similar) by mistake, but corrected it.

Deus eus The scribe wrote the abbreviated form "[d]s̄". temptauit temptauit? It seems that something was erased after the word "temptauit", but this remains uncertain due to the poor quality of the digital reproduction.
abraham in isaac
Defuncta efuncta est sarra saraa sarraa et emit locum abraham ad sepulchrum sepulcrum Φ Tu vs Am1 ab efron ephron

Praecepit precepit precepit abraham The addition in Am1, made - as it seems - by a corrector, was likely intended to clarify the laconic heading by specifying the subject of the first verb - Abraham, exactly -, whereas the subject of profectus est and adduxit is the servant. rȩcepit seruo et profectus est et adduxit rebeccam

Vixit ixit abraham annis centum C septuaginta LXX octoginta
quinque V quinqne

app.witDetail: The rubricator inserted n° XLVII twice - both in conjunction with n° XLVI and before the start of ch. 47 - likely to emphasize the presence of this chapter heading within the same line as the preceding one.

item.title: Generationes enerationes isaac

app.witDetail: Ch. 48 runs over two lines; the rubricator mistakenly numbered the second line as well (see below).

item.title:
Facta acta est fames famis fames famis Φ praeter Z (adiaph.) vs Am1 a.c. Tu et uisus est dominus isaac et dixit dominus dixit ei noli descendere desendere discendere descend?re Φ P8? (recte) vs Am1 vs Tu in aegyptum egiptum egyptum aegypto a????tum et habitauit in geraris
ubi seminauit et fecit centuplum The reading is added by a later hand in the blank space at the end of the chapter heading. ubi seminauit et fecit C ubi seminauit et fecit centuplum ubi seminauit et inuenit centuplum The chapter heading corresponds almost literally to the Old Latin text, which - according to Fischer (Vetus Latina: die Reste..., Vol. 2 Genesis, Freiburg, 1951-1954) - circulated in Italy: Gn 26:1 facta est autem fames, 26:2 apparuit (var. visus est) autem illi dominus et dixit noli descendere in aegyptum, 26:6 et habitavit isaac in geraris. Therefore, the section referring to Gn 26:12 seminauit autem isaac [...] et invenit (var. fecit) in anno illo centuplum hordei - preserved in five witnesses within the Turonian corpus and transmitted in a slightly altered form in the Tours Octateuch - should be considered part of the archetypal form of this heading. The omission that affects seven Turonian Bibles must have occurred early in the textual tradition, as the oldest manuscripts among them (codd. Sang. 75, Turon. 29, Paris. 11514) do not transmit it. The evidence from the Maurdramnus' Bible is less relevant, as its summary was added in the XII century; however, it appears to share the same tradition of omission found in the majority of the Turonian corpus, with the lost text having been restored by a corrector.

Senuit enuit isaac et nesciens benedixit bened??it It seems that some letters were erased in this reading and in the interlinear space, but this remains uncertain without firsthand examination. iacob e iacob The scribe may have initially begun to write "e[sau]" by mistake, but stopped to correct it.
pro esau et nesciuit et nessciuit
The alteration introduced by a corrector in Am1 - anticipating the participle nesciens instead of et nesciuit at the end - appears intended to refine the chapter heading's formulation as received by the manuscript tradition, rephrasing it to better match the event sequence of the biblical narration (Vulg. Gn 27:1 et non cognovit eum [...] benedicens ergo illi ait).
Dimisit imisit disit dimisit isaac iacob filium suum ut iret in mesopotamiam

Vidit idit iacob uisum et dominus locutus loquutus
est ad eum et benedixit eum ei
Venit enit iacob in orientem et accepit filias laban sibi s??i uxores

Peperit eperit lia iacob iacob lia liacob lia The scribe inserted the letter -l in the interlinear space between "peperit" and "iacob", but the correction is unclear.
uiro suo filios quattuor IIII IIIIor qua???or et stetit a
et stetit a partu pirtu partu p????
partu

Rachel achel dedit uiro suo The addition in the Maurdramnus' Bible appears to be a singular innovation of this codex, likely resulting from the interference of a similar phrase in the preceding chapter heading (ch. 53 iacob uiro suo). Φ Tu (adiaph.) vs Am1 iacob ancillam anchillam Φ Tu (recte) vs Am1 suam balam
Φ praeter P1 + Am1 (recte) vs Tu et peperit ei duos filios
et dinam See below, ch. 55 and note.

app.witDetail: Ch. 55 runs over two lines, the rubricator mistakenly numbered the second line as well; the incorrect numerals were erased by a later hand, which then wrote "vacat" twice above the erasure.

app.note: The variant ei (instead of illi in the Turonian Bibles and in the Tours Octateuch), chosen by D. de Bruyne in his edition, is attested - among other manuscripts - by the Bible of San Paolo fuori le mura in Rome.
The scribe of the Maurdramnus' Bible inadvertently skipped the entire chapter heading, instead placing the reading et dinam at the end of the preceding one (see above, ch. 54).

item.title:
Memoratus emoratus est
Φ Tu (fort. recte) vs Am1 deus dominus dominus deus The Old Latin text attested by Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100) reads deus (Gn 30:22 memor factus est autem deus), which matches the Greek ὁ θεός. The variant dominus may have originated independently in the Maurdramnus' Bible and in the cod. Turon. 29, whose scribes likely confused the abbreviated forms ds̄ and dn̄s. Φ Tr p.c. Tu (adiaph.) vs Am1 Tr a.c. rachel et peperit ioseph et statuit ?? s?????t
iacob Φ Tu (error) vs Am1 cum
The archetypical form of this chapter heading should combine the reading of the Maurdramnus' Bible et statuit iacob with the Turonian Bibles' and Tours Octateuch's cum Laban. Φ Tu (recte) vs Am1 laban de ouibus uariis vari?s
Dixit ixit deus iacob reuertere in terram patris tui et consecutus consecutum consequutus Φ Tu (recte) vs Am1 est eum laban ubi iurauerunt inuicem

Vidit idit uubi uubdit iacob castra astra Φ Tu (recte) vs Am1 dei et misit nuntios nontios Φ Tu (recte) vs Am1 ad fratrem suum
et occurrit ei It is uncertain whether the addition in the Maurdramnus' Bible should be considered part of the chapter heading's archetypical form, as the summary was integrated in the XII century. Nevertheless, the addition's phrasing more closely resembles that of the Old Latin version in Codex Lugdunensis (Gn. 33:4 et accucurrit esau in oviam fratri suo) than the parallel passage in the Vulgate (currens itaque esau obviam fratri suo), even though the subject of the biblical narrative is Esau.
Venit enit iacob in ciuitatem sicimorum sicymorum sycimorum sichimorum ubi occiderunt filii eius ei?? omnes inhabitantes habitantes in habitantes Φ Tu (recte) vs Am1 propter dinam di??nam dinam

app.witDetail: The scribe likely confused the abbreviated forms "ds̄" and "dn̄s".

app.note: The reading chosen by D. de Bruyne in his edition aligns with the Old Latin text reconstructed by Fischer (Vetus Latina: die Reste..., Vol. 2 Genesis, Freiburg, 1951-1954): (Gn 35:1) dixit autem deus ad iacob (cf. the Septuagint: εἶπεν δὲ ὁ θεὸς πρὸς Ιακωβ).

title.q: ascende in locum bethel betehl Φ Tu vs Am1

item.title:
Visus isus est dominus ipsi iacob et benedixit eum cognominauit et cognominauit cognominauitque The text transmitted almost unanimously by the Turonian Bibles is acceptable, though syntactically quite harsh. Since the readings of the Maurdramnus' Bible and the Tours Octateuch differ, the variant may represent an intentional smoothing of the asyndeton; the singular reading of K within the Turonian corpus seems to confirm this hypothesis. According to Fischer (Vetus Latina: die Reste..., Vol. 2 Genesis, Freiburg, 1951-1954), Hieronymus' Quaestiones hebraicae in Genesim (P. L. XXIII, 991) is the unique source of the Old Latin version (Gn 35:10 et vocavit nomen eius israhel), which corresponds to a Greek text marked with Hexaplaric asterisks (καὶ ἐκάλεσεν...). However, the matching of this Old Latin version with the Maurdramnus' Bible is in all probability fortuitous.
Φ praeter K vs Am1 vs Tu eum israhel
Vbi bi peperit rachel racehl Φ Tu vs Am1 beniamin et mortua est et e? de generationibus generationib?? iacob
De e generationibus esau

app.witDetail: The scribe wrote the numeral "64" twice, before and after the chapter heading.

item.title: De e principibus princib principibus The scribe nearly wrote "princib[us]" by mistake, but stopped mid-word to correct it. princibus esau
Qui ui regnauerunt in edom priusquam priusquae esset rex in israhel e israhel The scribe seems to have begun writing "e[dom]" erroneously a second time, but stopped to correct the text.

De e ioseph et de
Φ praeter K + Tu (recte) vs Am1 somniis somnis Φ Tu (recte) vs Am1 eius

Vbi bi missus est
Φ Tu (fort. recte) vs Am1 in puteum poteum et distractus est ismahelitis ismahelis ismaelitis
De e iuda et thamar tamar Φ Am1 vs Tu uxore filii eius quae que qui Φ praeter Z + Am1 (recte) vs Tu erat uidua

app.lem:
De e ioseph et
et uxore uxorem Φ Am1 (recte) vs Tu putifare petefre petefre
putaphare According to Fischer (Vetus Latina: die Reste..., Vol. 2 Genesis, Freiburg, 1951-1954), both variant readings appear to date back to Old Latin versions. The form petephres - attested (with a slight orthographical variation) by the Maurdramnus' Bible, the Tours Octateuch, and two of the earliest Turonian Bibles (codd. Sang. 75 and Paris. 11514) - is the one Augustine quotes in his Quaestiones in Genesim, 130, and is the form that aligns with the Septuagint Πετεφρης. In contrast, putiphares is attested by the apocryphal Hypomnesticon Augustini contra Pelagianos et Caelestianos haereticos, III 23 (P. L. XLV, 1633). This latter form is closer to the one adopted by Jerome in the Vulgate (Gn 39:1 emitque eum putiphar) and to the one found in the majority of witnesses within the Turonian corpus.
app.note: In cod. Sang. 75 a corrector (likely the same who corrected the chapter numbering errors) struck out the traditional heading and added a new one that finds no parallel in any of the summaries known to D. de Bruyne. The new heading echoes the text of the Vulgate quite closely (Gn 39:1 igitur ioseph ductus est in aegyptum).

title.note: Φ praeter Sg1 P8 Z vs Am1 Tu

item.title:
De e his is his qui in carcerem carcere carcere somnia uiderunt et interpraetauit Written with the abbreviated form -p̄ interpretauit interpraetatione The corrector wrote the reading with the abbreviated form -p̄. interpretauit interptauit The scribe inadvertently omitted the abbreviation mark. Φ praeter P2 K vs Am1 a.c.? Tu ea ioseph
The variant interpraetatione in the Maurdramnus' Bible may be the result of a corrector's conjectural attempt to amend the incomplete heading, which was corrupt due to the loss of the subject and object of interpretavit (cf. the Vulgate at Gn 40:12 haec est interpretatio somnii).
Somnium omnium pharaonis quod interpraetauit Written with the abbreviated form -p̄. interpretauit Φ praeter P2 L1 K vs Am1 Tu ioseph

Vbi bi misit iacob filios suos in aegyptum egytum ægyptum
in egytum filios suos Φ Tu (adiaph.) vs Am1 et cognouit eos ioseph et unum ex ipsis reclusit usque dum ueniret ueniret et Φ Tu (recte) vs Am1 frater eius beniamin beniam??
Inualuit nualuit fames famis et uenerunt ad eum cum beniamin et mandauit patri suo uenire ad se

Visus isus est dominus
dominus iacob et dixit illi descende discende desc?nde The scribe left a blank space (ca. 3 letters) after "descende", there appears to be an erasure; a horizontal line is added to confirm no omission. Φ Am1 vs Tu in aegyptum egyptum aegiptum aegyptum aeg?ptum

app.witDetail: The rubricator was about to write erroneously "LXXV[I]", but stopped it (only the top of the line is drawn).

item.title: Cum um septuaginta quinque LXXV LXX The number of people who entered Egypt with Jacob is given as seventy-five in the Old Latin text transmitted by the Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100): Gn 46:27 omnes ergo animae quae introierunt in aegypto LXXV, which corresponds to the Septuagint ἑβδομήκοντα πέντε; conversely, the Vulgate gives the number as seventy. Since the manuscript tradition for this chapter heading is unanimous, the variant in B2 is in all probability an independent error, unrelated to the Vulgate reading. animabus intrauit iacob in aegyptum egyptum aeg?ptum
Venit enit obuiam ioseph israhel israhl israhel patri suo
Nuntiauit untiauit ioseph pharaoni quoniam uenit uenisset Φ Tu (adiaph.) vs Am1 pater eius

Dixit ixit pharao ph?rao pha??o ioseph ut eligeret
eligeret elegeret Φ praeter Sg1 P8 Z P2 + Am1 p.c. (recte) vs Tu terram terra in qua habitaret pater pa??? p???? eius

Habitauit abitauit israhel in aegypto egyptum aegyptum Φ (recte) vs Am1 Tu et dixit filio suo ioseph
Φ Tu (recte) vs Am1 ne sepeliret sepelil sepeliret The scribe may have initially begun to write "sepelil[iret]" by mistake, but stopped to correct it. se??l???? eum illic uixit ixit
autem iacob
annis annos annis annos The abbreviation "ann̄s" is ambiguous, but see below, ch. 82. In Classical Latin duration is expressed using the accusative case. However, the ablative annis of the Tours Octateuch and most Turonian Bibles is found within the narrative passage to which the chapter heading refers, as attested in both the Codex Lugdunensis (VL100): Gn 47:28 superuixit autem iacob in terra aegypti annis XVII, and in the Vulgate: et vixit in ea decem et septem annis. Φ praeter Tr Mb2 p.c. B2 P1 K + Tu (adiaph.) vs Am1 centum quadraginta septem CXLV?? CXLVII CXL?II centum quadraginta se???? CXLVIII centum quadraginta octo Jacob's age is numbered as 146 in the Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100), whereas the Vulgate gives it as 147, a reading that agrees with the Septuagint. The figure 148 is unsupported by any other textual witness and must therefore be deemed a unique error of Z and K.
Et t infirmatus est iacob et benedixit filios ioseph iosef ioseph alternis manibus
Vocauit ocauit iacob iacb omnes filios suos et dixit eis quae que quȩ quæ uentura uen???? essent in nouissimis diebus et defunctus est

Vixit ixit ioseph annis annos annos annis Regarding the variant readings annis and annos, see the critical note at ch. 79. The ablative is the reading found in both the Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100) and the Vulgate at Gn 50:22. Φ praeter P8 a.c. P1 + Tu (adiaph.) vs Am1 centum decim CX centum decem centum et decem
CX annos The word order in the Tours Octateuch and the Turonian Bibles mirrors the Old Latin tradition of Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100): Gn 50:22 et vixit ioseph annis CX, while the Maurdramnus' Bible more closely follows the Vulgate: vixitque centum decem annis.
Φ Tu (adiaph.) vs Am1 et adiurauit fratres fra???s
The heading combines two passages from Gn 50:23-25: et dixit ioseph fratribus suis [...] et adiuravit ioseph filios israhel in the Codex Lugdunensis (VL 100), locutus est fratribus suis [...] cumque adiurasset eos in the Vulgate. However, the omission of the reading fratres - unanimously attested in the manuscript tradition - in the edition of D. de Bruyne should be considered an oversight.
suos ut cum cu? uisitarentur tollerent secum ossa eius
eius in ?n terram suam

list.trailer:
EXPLICIVNT CAPITVLA
EXPLiciunt CAPitula EXPLiciuNT CAPITuLa EXPLICVERVNT CAPITVLA EXPLICIVNT CAPITVLA EXPLICIVNT CAPITVLA EXPLICVERVNT CAPITVLA IN LIBRO GENESEOS EXPLICVERUNT CAPITVLA IN LIBRO GENESEOS EXPLiciuNT CAPITVLA EXPLICVERunt CAPiTuLa
